message.content: Adversaries may exploit TFTP Boot (netbooting) to load unauthorized operating systems on network devices by manipulating the boot configuration to use a malicious TFTP server. This technique leverages the standard functionality of netbooting, commonly used by administrators for centralized management of device images. By specifying a rogue TFTP server, attackers can load a modified system image during device startup or reset, enabling them to alter device configurations, introduce malicious capabilities, and establish backdoors. This method, akin to a ROMMONkit, allows adversaries to maintain control over the network device with minimal detection. Mitigation strategies include securing boot configurations, restricting TFTP server access, monitoring for unauthorized configuration changes, and employing integrity checks on system images to detect tampering.
